title: crossLagR
text:
Preface
The crossLagR package provides tools for estimating, simulating, and evaluating cross-lagged panel models in R. It supports a range of structural equation models for panel data, including the standard Cross-Lagged Panel Model (CLPM), the Random Intercept CLPM (RI-CLPM), the Bollen and Brand dynamic panel model, latent change score models, and latent growth models.
The package is available on GitHub:

title: crossLagR
text:
Motivation
The Cross-Lagged Panel Model (CLPM) is a widely used method for examining dynamic relationships in panel data. However, it is susceptible to unaccounted time-invariant confounders and measurement error, leading to spurious estimates of autoregressive and cross-lagged relationships. Both the RI-CLPM and the Latent Change Model (LCM) extend the standard CLPM by explicitly modeling unit-level effects — stable individual differences that are constant across time. In the RI-CLPM, this is represented by a random intercept factor, while in the LCM, it is captured through a constant change factor.

title: crossLagR
text:
Organization
This guide is organized as follows:

Theoretical Background. An overview of the CLPM, RI-CLPM, LCM, LGM, and related models, including their mathematical formulations and the sources of bias in the standard CLPM. The chapter concludes with a unified framework (Usami, Murayama, and Hamaker 2019) that organizes all models as special cases sharing common parameter types (ar_x, ar_y, cl_xy, cl_yx, etc.).
Using crossLagR. A walkthrough of the package’s estimation, simulation, and Monte Carlo functions, with path diagrams and worked examples using simulated data. All estimation functions use the unified labeling convention.
Monte Carlo Simulations. Compares estimator performance (CLPM, RI-CLPM, Bollen & Brand, Latent Change) under varying degrees of unobserved heterogeneity using 4-wave panel data.
Applied Example. An empirical application using the Institute for the Study of Citizens and Politics (ISCAP) Panel, examining the relationship between political legitimacy and partisanship from 2012–2020.





Usami, Satoshi, Kou Murayama, and Ellen L. Hamaker. 2019. “A Unified Framework of Longitudinal Models to Examine Reciprocal Relations.” Psychological Methods 24 (5): 637–57. https://doi.org/10.1037/met0000210.

title: 1  Installation and Package Syntax
text:
1.1 Installation
crossLagR can be found on GitHub and is not yet on CRAN. Install with devtools (or remotes):
The package is generally just a wrapper around lavaan for SEM estimation, MASS for multivariate normal draws in some simulators, and the usual tidyverse tools (dplyr, tidyr, tibble, purrr, stringr) for data wrangling. DiagrammeR is used to render the path diagrams reproduced throughout this guide. All dependencies are listed in the DESCRIPTION file and will be installed automatically with the above command.
library(crossLagR)
library(lavaan)
library(dplyr)

title: 1  Installation and Package Syntax
text:
# install.packages("devtools")
devtools::install_github("crweber9874/crossLagR")

title: 1  Installation and Package Syntax
text:
1.2 Package Conventions
The way the functions are written is that they fall into three families, identifiable by their prefix:

estimate*() — generate lavaan model syntax. Each returns a character string that can be fed directly into lavaan::lavaan() or lavaan::sem(). So estimateRICLPM(waves = 4) returns a string of lavaan syntax for a 4-wave RI-CLPM, for instance.
sim*() — simulate data from a specified DGP. Each returns a list with $model (the population lavaan syntax) and $data (a wide-format tibble with one row per unit and columns x1, …, xT, y1, …, yT).
monteCarlo*() — wrappers that repeat a simulate-then-fit cycle and return a tibble table of results.

All estimate*() functions in the unified family share a common labeling convention so that estimates from different models can be lined up side-by-side. The convention is to generally follow the framework introduced by Usami, Murayama, and Hamaker (2019):

Unified parameter labels in crossLagR. The substantive interpretation depends on the model — see Chapter 4 — but the labels themselves are stable across estimators. An AR parameter in one model may not mean the same as an AR parameter in another, but they will both be labeled ar_x or ar_y in the output.







Label
Meaning
Used in




ar_x, ar_y
Autoregressive parameters
every estimator


cl_xy, cl_yx
Cross-lagged parameters
every estimator


I_x, I_y
Trait / intercept factor
RI-CLPM, B&B, ALT, LCM-SR


S_x, S_y

ALT, LCM-SR, LGM

Accumulating change factor
LCSM


u_var_x, u_var_y

title: 1  Installation and Package Syntax
text:
1.3 Common Arguments
Every estimate*() accepts at least:

waves — number of time points. Lower bound depends on the model (2 for CLPM, 3 for most others, 4 for estimate_change_to_change).
constrain_beta — equality constraint across waves on AR parameters. Default TRUE.
constrain_omega — equality constraint across waves on CL parameters. Default TRUE.

Bivariate/multivariate models add:

variable_type = c("univariate", "bivariate") — for estimateLChange() and estimateLGM().
include_z = TRUE — for estimateRICLPM(); adds a third process.
constrain_residual_variances, constrain_residual_covariances — equality constraints on innovation variances and within-wave covariances.

Sim wrappers accept the same constraint arguments plus true population values: beta_x/beta_y (AR), omega_xy/omega_yx (CL), and variance components specific to each model (e.g. var_BX, var_BY for RI-CLPM trait variances, var_p, var_q for within-person dynamic-residual variances).






NoteSim/estimate argument names differ from output labels



For historical reasons the sim*() true-value arguments use beta_* and omega_*, but the estimate*() syntax labels its parameters ar_* and cl_*. This is intentional — beta / omega refer to the DGP’s true values, while ar / cl refer to estimates from any model in the family.

title: 1  Installation and Package Syntax
text:
1.4 Estimator Quick Reference

# CLPM — baseline cross-lagged panel
estimateCLPM(waves = 4)

# RI-CLPM — random intercept + within-person AR/CL
estimateRICLPM(waves = 4)

# Latent Change — proportional change with optional accumulating factor
estimateLChange(waves = 4, variable_type = "bivariate",
                estimate_constant_change = TRUE)

# Linear Growth — intercept + slope, no dynamics
estimateLGM(waves = 4, variable_type = "bivariate")

# ALT — growth + AR/CL on observed scores (compounding)
estimateALT(waves = 4)

# LCM-SR / GCLM — growth + AR/CL on structured residuals
estimateLCMSR(waves = 4)

# Bollen & Brand — latent fixed effects dynamic panel
estimateBollen_and_Brand(waves = 4)

# Trait-State-Occasion — three-component variance decomposition
estimateTSO(waves = 4)

Each call returns a character string. To fit, pipe it into lavaan:

fit <- lavaan(estimateRICLPM(waves = 4), data = my_panel)
summary(fit, standardized = TRUE)

title: 1  Installation and Package Syntax
text:
1.5 Simulation Quick Reference

# CLPM with two waves
sim <- simCLPM(waves = 4, beta_x = 0.5, beta_y = 0.5,
               omega_xy = 0.2, omega_yx = 0.1,
               sample.nobs = 1000)

# RI-CLPM with traits
sim <- simRICLPM(waves = 4, beta_x = 0.3, beta_y = 0.3,
                 omega_xy = 0.2, omega_yx = 0.1,
                 var_BX = 1, var_BY = 1,
                 sample.nobs = 1000)

# Latent change
sim <- simLChange(waves = 5, variable_type = "bivariate",
                  beta_x = -0.5, beta_y = -0.5,
                  omega_x = 0.3, omega_y = 0.3,
                  sample.nobs = 1000)

# Wide tibble: id, x1..xT, y1..yT
str(sim$data, list.len = 6)

The wide-format output is what every estimate*() expects when fed to lavaan. A long-format helper reshape_long_sim_cr() is available for plotting, but generally the convention here is to keep the data in wide format.

title: 1  Installation and Package Syntax
text:
1.6 Quick

set.seed(2026)

# Simulate from an RI-CLPM
sim <- simRICLPM(waves       = 5,
                 beta_x      = 0.3,
                 beta_y      = 0.3,
                 omega_xy    = 0.2,
                 omega_yx    = 0.1,
                 var_BX      = 1,
                 var_BY      = 1,
                 sample.nobs = 1000)

# Fit the RI-CLPM
fit <- lavaan(estimateRICLPM(waves = 5), data = sim$data)

# Pull just the AR and CL parameters
parameterEstimates(fit) |>
  dplyr::filter(label %in% c("ar_x", "ar_y", "cl_xy", "cl_yx")) |>
  dplyr::select(label, est, se, pvalue) |>
  dplyr::mutate(across(where(is.numeric), \(x) round(x, 3)))

The same labels (ar_x, cl_yx, …) will appear in the parameterEstimates() output regardless of which estimate*() function generated the syntax — that’s what makes the unified framing useful when comparing estimators on the same data.

Source code is on GitHub at https://github.com/crweber9874/crossLagR; issues and feature requests welcome.

title: 2  Revisiting the Cross-Lagged Panel Model
text:
2.0.1 The CLPM Diagram
Social scientists often turn to panel data to diagnose causal effects when experiments are impractical (Chiu et al. 2025). Whereas dozens of options exist for analyzing longer time series, researchers wishing to analyze cross-sectionally dominant panel structures – in which there are more units than time points – face a more limited set of choices. National surveys like found in the American National Election Studies (ANES) may consist of just a handful of waves, with many respondents interviewed only once or twice.
We consider of the popular methods for analyzing panel data in political science. We start by focusing on a common approach to estimate dynamic processes in panel data (particularly public opinion panel data that are “cross section” dominant, with far more respondents than panel waves: the cross-lagged panel model (CLPM). The CLPM – introduced by Stanley and Campbell (1963) and extended on by Kenny (1975) – has been widely used to study reciprocal relationships between variables over time. These methods first appeared in the political science literature in the late 1970s Meier and Campbell (1979). The availability of two large panel surveys with rich political content – Jennings and Niemi’s Jennings and Niemi (1974) socialization study and the ANES 1972-1974-1976 panel – likely promoted the CLPM’s adoption, as most of the cross-lagged analyses published in this era analyze these two datasets. Another factor leading to the widespread adoption of the CLPM was the availability of Jöreskog’s LISREL software, which allowed researchers to quickly and easily solve systems of equations using maximum likelihood (Markus 1979a; McCullough 1978; Shingles 1976).
Much of the CLPM’s appeal lies in its intuitive structure, flexibility, and ease of implementation. In the half century after its introduction, the CLPM has also been refined and extended, due to important limitations in its original formulation. For instance, the model can account for unobserved heterogeneity across individuals – stable characteristics of individuals that confound the “dynamic” estimates in the CLPM (Usami, Hayes, and McArdle 2015; Usami 2021; Klopack and Wickrama 2020; Kim and Steiner 2021; Lüdtke and Robitzsch 2022; McArdle 2009). Because the CLPM does not account for stable unit effects, the cross-lagged estimates will tend to be biased (upwards), as they conflate within-person dynamics with between-person differences. Though not directly referenced as a critique of the CLPM, Hsiao (2022) shows that including a lagged dependent variable will produce a biased estimate of the lagged effect, because the variable is correlated with the error term. The correlation arises from the failure to account for “between unit” heterogeneity (pp. 64 -67). As a consequence, CLPM estimates may be biased, especially when the variables of interest exhibit high stability over time (Hamaker, Kuiper, and Grasman 2015; Usami, Hayes, and McArdle 2015; Lüdtke and Robitzsch 2022).
This problem is elaborated on in Hsiao (2022). Dynamic models – models that include lagged dependent variables – often result in correlations between the observed variables and the error terms, leading to biased estimates. Hsiao (2022) proposes and instrumental variable approach using lagged values of the dependent variable as instruments. When stable unit effects are ignored, the lagged dependent variable becomes correlated with the error term, violating the exogeneity assumption. This correlation leads to biased estimates of the lagged effect.
Another popular extension to the CLPM is the random intercept cross-lagged panel model (RI-CLPM) proposed in Hamaker, Kuiper, and Grasman (2015), which separates within-person dynamics from between-person “trait” effects (Usami, Hayes, and McArdle 2015; Lüdtke and Robitzsch 2022). Another approach is the Latent Change Score Model (LSCM), which focuses more directly on estimating different change processes; the LCSM also accounts for stable unit effects, by accounting for unit averaged change (Usami, Hayes, and McArdle 2015; McArdle 2009; Kim and Steiner 2021). Another alternative is the latent growth model (LGM), which models individual trajectories over time (Usami, Hayes, and McArdle 2015; McArdle and Nesselroade 2003).
Ultimately,preference for one model over another is strongly tied to data access and availability. While the CLPM and a restrictive version of the LCSM can be estimated with as few as two waves of data, the RI-CLPM, the Latent Growth Model, and Latent Change Score Model require more panel waves to properly identify the models. Data with three or more waves of data afford more leverage to estimate dynamic relationships. In this project, we elaborate on these models, beginning with the CLPM and change score approach to modeling dynamic relationships.
The CLPM can be represented as a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) Figure 2.1.
Figure 2.1: The Cross-Lagged Panel Model. The DAG displays cross-lagged and autoregressive paths between two variables over three time points.
The CLPM – a dynamic panel model – leverages the temporal ordering of variables to estimate how one variable leads to change in another variable. Does the lagged effect of \(y\) on \(x\) exert a stronger influence than the lagged effect of \(x\) on \(y\)? Is one variable causally dominant (for a critique, see (Rogosa 1980)? Alternatively, contrasting the the cross-lagged effects is also critical to establishing Granger Causality (Granger 1969).
A crucial limitation of the CLPM is that it does not account for stable trait-like differences between individuals. Consider a simple example, the relationship between a personality characteristic, openness versus closed psychological orientation, and political attitudes, a right-wing political orientation Luttig (2021). Bakker, Lelkes, and Malka (2021) for instance, examines whether a psychological disposition towards openness predicts a right-wing political orientation, or whether a right-wing political orientation predicts a psychological disposition towards openness.
Political orientations are generally stable (Jennings and Niemi 1975; Goren 2005; Ansolabehere, Rodden, and Snyder 2008). It is also well regarded that personality traits are stable over time, and partially heritable (Costa and McCrae 1988; Roberts, Walton, and Viechtbauer 2006; Hatemi and Verhulst 2015).
The CLPM does not account for this time-invariant stability. It implicitly assumes that all variation in the observed variables is time-varying, and that there are no stable, time-invariant components.
Following Hamaker, Kuiper, and Grasman (2015) and Usami (2021), the observed variables might be further decomposed into a time-invariant trait component and a time-varying component.
\[
\begin{eqnarray}
x_{it} = \mu_{xt} + I_{x,i} + x^*_{x,it} \\
y_{it} = \mu_{yt} + I_{y,i} + y^*_{y,it}
\end{eqnarray}
\]
where \(\mu_{xt}\) and \(\mu_{yt}\) are time-specific means, and \(x^*_{it}\) and \(y^*_{it}\) are person-specific deviations from those time-specific means (Usami, Murayama, and Hamaker 2019; Usami, Hayes, and McArdle 2015; Usami 2021).
Figure 2.2 provides a visual representation of a modified version of the CLPM with time-invariant trait components.
Figure 2.2: The Cross-Lagged Panel Model with Stable, Time-Invariant Unit Factors. The DAG displays cross-lagged and autoregressive paths between two variables over three time points. Intercepts, \(I\) vary across respondents but not time.
It is useful to ask the question: What if one fits Figure 2.1 but Figure 2.2 is the true population model. Assume the following model is estimated.
\[
\begin{align}
x_{it} &=  b^{CLPM}_{xx}\,x_{i,t-1} + b^{CLPM}_{yx}\,y_{i,t-1} + e_{x,it} \\
y_{it} &=  b^{CLPM}_{yy}\,y_{i,t-1} + b^{CLPM}_{xy}\,x_{i,t-1} + e_{y,it}
\end{align}
\]
The conventional interpretation of the CLPM coefficients are that \(\beta_{xx}\), captures the autoregressive effect of \(x\) on itself, and \(\beta_{yy}\) does the same for \(y\). On the other hand, \(\beta_{yx}\) captures the cross-lagged effect of \(y\) on \(x\), and \(\beta_{xy}\) captures the cross-lagged effect of \(x\) on \(y\).
If Figure 2.2 captures the true data-generating process, the above intepretation of the CLPM parameters is incorrect.The degree of bias in the parameters should be proportional to the size of the trait component. In Figure 2.2, both \(I_x\) and \(I_y\) represent the time invariant trait components of the observed measures. The variance of \(x\) and \(y\) can be decomposed into a between-person component (the trait) and a within-person component (the deviation from the trait).
\[
\begin{aligned}
  {Var}(x_{it}) &= \text{Var}(I_{x,i}) + \text{Var}(x^*_{x,it})\\
  {Var}(y_{it}) &= \text{Var}(I_{y,i}) + \text{Var}(y^*_{y,it})
\end{aligned}
\]
\[
\begin{align}
x^*_{it} &=  b^{True}_{xx}\,x^*_{i,t-1} + b^{True}_{yx}\,y^*_{i,t-1} + e_{x,it} \\
y^*_{it} &=  b^{True}_{yy}\,y^*_{i,t-1} + b^{True}_{xy}\,x^*_{i,t-1} + e_{y,it}
\end{align}
\]
Importantly, the percentage of the total variance attributable to the between-person component is the intraclass correlation: \(\text{ICC}_x \equiv \sigma^2_{I_x}/(\sigma^2_{I_x} + \sigma^2_{x^*})\), with \(\text{ICC}_y\) defined analogously.
The problem with fitting the CLPM to data generated by Figure 2.2 is that a non-zero covariance forms between the lagged regressors and the error terms. This is because the lagged regressor contains the trait component. The intuition is easiest to see through univariate estimates, estimating the cross-lagged and autoregressive effects individually — what one would estimate from \(x_t\) on \(x_{t-1}\) alone, or \(x_t\) on \(y_{t-1}\) alone..
\[
b_{xx}^{\text{univariate}}
\equiv \frac{\text{Cov}(x_t, x_{t-1})}{\text{Var}(x_{t-1})}
= \frac{\sigma^2_{I_x} + b^{\text{true}}_{xx}\,\sigma^2_{x^*}}{\sigma^2_{I_x} + \sigma^2_{x^*}}
= \text{ICC}_x + (1-\text{ICC}_x)\,b^{\text{true}}_{xx},
\]
\[
b_{yx}^{\text{univariate}}
\equiv \frac{\text{Cov}(x_t, y_{t-1})}{\text{Var}(y_{t-1})}
= \frac{\sigma_{I_xI_y} + b^{\text{true}}_{yx}\,\sigma^2_{y^*}}{\sigma^2_{I_y} + \sigma^2_{y^*}}
= \frac{\sigma_{I_xI_y}}{\sigma^2_{I_y} + \sigma^2_{y^*}} + (1-\text{ICC}_y)\,b^{\text{true}}_{yx}.
\]
If the true autoregressive effect is zero (\(b^{\text{true}}_{xx}=0\)), then \(b_{xx}^{\text{univariate}} = \text{ICC}_x\) — the CLPM picks up a spurious AR equal to the ICC. If the true cross-lagged effect is zero (\(b^{\text{true}}_{yx}=0\)), then \(b_{yx}^{\text{univariate}} = \sigma_{I_xI_y}/(\sigma^2_{I_y}+\sigma^2_{y^*})\) — a spurious cross-lag driven entirely by the trait covariance. If the trait covariance is also zero (\(\sigma_{I_xI_y}=0\)), then \(b_{yx}^{\text{univariate}} = (1-\text{ICC}_y)\,b^{\text{true}}_{yx}\), i.e. attenuated by the share of variance that is between-person.
The multivariate CLPM includes both lags simultaneously, so its coefficients are partial slopes rather than these simple ratios. But the general intuition still holds. When \(\sigma^2_{I_x} > 0\), the AR is inflated by the trait variance, and the CL is contaminated by the trait covariance. When \(\sigma^2_{I_x} = 0\), there is no bias in the AR, but the CL is still attenuated by the between-person variance in \(y\).

crumbs: 2  Revisiting the Cross-Lagged Panel Model

title: 3  Latent Change Score Models
text:
3.1 The LCSM
Whereas the previous chapter established dynamic lagged and cross lagged effects in level form (the CLPM and its bias under unit heterogeneity), this chapter turns to the change representation. By “differencing” the observed scores, this effectively absorbs all time-invariant heterogeneity, including stable between-person differences.
The Latent Change Score Model (LCSM) parameterizes dynamics differently as differences across waves (McArdle 2009; Kim and Steiner 2021). The logic is to remove out any stable respondent effects by simply calculating the wave to wave difference. The LCSM uses a measurement equation that separates latent true scores from unique factors, and a dynamic equation that maps lagged true scores onto latent change scores via a proportional self-feedback parameter (\(\beta\), i.e. ar_x) and a coupling parameter (\(\omega\), i.e. cl_xy). Finally, a second-order constant change factor can be specified which accumulates over waves, though requires 4+ waves for identification. This term captures stable, person-specific trajectories that are not accounted for by differencing.
Figure 3.1 sketches the bivariate LCSM with a constant-change factor, in the same DAG style introduced in the previous chapter. Each observed score is decomposed as \(X_t = X_{t-1} + \Delta x_t\); each change score is regressed on prior levels via proportional change (\(\beta\)) and coupling (\(\omega\)), and accumulates a person-specific constant (\(\Delta_x\), \(\Delta_y\)). The detailed measurement-model version with multiple indicators is reproduced in the appendix as Figure 3.2.
Figure 3.1: Bivariate Latent Change Score Model. β = proportional change; ω = coupling. Δ_x, Δ_y are person-specific constant-change factors loading on each Δx_t and Δy_t.

title: 3  Latent Change Score Models
text:
3.2 estimateLChange(): Generated lavaan Syntax
estimateLChange() returns a lavaan model string with the unified labels: ar_x, ar_y are proportional self-feedback (the effective autoregressive parameter is \(1+AR()\)); cl_xy, cl_yx are coupling (cross-lagged) parameters; A_x, A_y are accumulating constant-change factors; u_var_x, u_var_y are unique-factor (measurement-error) variances. The function returns syntax that can be passed directly to lavaan::lavaan().

3.2.1 Univariate LCSM with constant change
The simplest specification: one variable, four waves, constant-change factor switched to on.

estimateLChange(waves                    = 4,
                variable_type            = "univariate",
                estimate_constant_change = TRUE) |>
  cat()

    cf1 =~ 1*x1
    cf2 =~ 1*x2
    cf3 =~ 1*x3
    cf4 =~ 1*x4
    cf1 ~ 1
    cf2 ~ 0*1
    cf3 ~ 0*1
    cf4 ~ 0*1
    cf1 ~~ cf1
    cf2 ~~ 0*cf2
    cf3 ~~ 0*cf3
    cf4 ~~ 0*cf4
    x1 ~ 0*1
    x2 ~ 0*1
    x3 ~ 0*1
    x4 ~ 0*1
    x1 ~~ u_var_x*x1
    x2 ~~ u_var_x*x2
    x3 ~~ u_var_x*x3
    x4 ~~ u_var_x*x4
    cf2 ~ 1*cf1
    cf3 ~ 1*cf2
    cf4 ~ 1*cf3
    ld2 =~ 1*cf2
    ld3 =~ 1*cf3
    ld4 =~ 1*cf4
    ld2 ~ 0*1
    ld3 ~ 0*1
    ld4 ~ 0*1
    ld2 ~~ 0*ld2
    ld3 ~~ 0*ld3
    ld4 ~~ 0*ld4
    A_x =~ 1*ld2
          + 1*ld3
          + 1*ld4
    A_x ~ 1
    A_x ~~ A_x
    A_x ~~ cf1
    ld2 ~ ar_x*cf1
    ld3 ~ ar_x*cf2
    ld4 ~ ar_x*cf3




3.2.2 Bivariate LCSM with constant change
This adds the second process and the coupling parameters cl_xy, cl_yx. With constrain_beta = TRUE and constrain_omega = TRUE the proportional and coupling parameters are held equal across waves.

estimateLChange(waves                    = 4,
                variable_type            = "bivariate",
                constrain_beta           = TRUE,
                constrain_omega          = TRUE,
                estimate_constant_change = TRUE) |>
  cat()

    cf_x1 =~ 1*x1
    cf_x2 =~ 1*x2
    cf_x3 =~ 1*x3
    cf_x4 =~ 1*x4
    cf_x1 ~ 1
    cf_x2 ~ 0*1
    cf_x3 ~ 0*1
    cf_x4 ~ 0*1
    cf_x1 ~~ start(15)*cf_x1
    cf_x2 ~~ 0*cf_x2
    cf_x3 ~~ 0*cf_x3
    cf_x4 ~~ 0*cf_x4
    x1 ~ 0*1
    x2 ~ 0*1
    x3 ~ 0*1
    x4 ~ 0*1
    x1 ~~ u_var_x*x1
    x2 ~~ u_var_x*x2
    x3 ~~ u_var_x*x3
    x4 ~~ u_var_x*x4
    cf_x2 ~ 1*cf_x1
    cf_x3 ~ 1*cf_x2
    cf_x4 ~ 1*cf_x3
    ld_x2 =~ 1*cf_x2
    ld_x3 =~ 1*cf_x3
    ld_x4 =~ 1*cf_x4
    ld_x2 ~ 0*1
    ld_x3 ~ 0*1
    ld_x4 ~ 0*1
    ld_x2 ~~ 0*ld_x2
    ld_x3 ~~ 0*ld_x3
    ld_x4 ~~ 0*ld_x4
    A_x =~ 1*ld_x2
    + 1*ld_x3
    + 1*ld_x4
    A_x ~ 1
    A_x ~~ A_var_x*A_x
    A_x ~~ cf_x1
    ld_x2 ~ start(-0.15)*ar_x*cf_x1
    ld_x3 ~ start(-0.15)*ar_x*cf_x2
    ld_x4 ~ start(-0.15)*ar_x*cf_x3
    cf_y1 =~ 1*y1
    cf_y2 =~ 1*y2
    cf_y3 =~ 1*y3
    cf_y4 =~ 1*y4
    cf_y1 ~ 1
    cf_y2 ~ 0*1
    cf_y3 ~ 0*1
    cf_y4 ~ 0*1
    cf_y1 ~~ start(15)*cf_y1
    cf_y2 ~~ 0*cf_y2
    cf_y3 ~~ 0*cf_y3
    cf_y4 ~~ 0*cf_y4
    y1 ~ 0*1
    y2 ~ 0*1
    y3 ~ 0*1
    y4 ~ 0*1
    y1 ~~ u_var_y*y1
    y2 ~~ u_var_y*y2
    y3 ~~ u_var_y*y3
    y4 ~~ u_var_y*y4
    cf_y2 ~ 1*cf_y1
    cf_y3 ~ 1*cf_y2
    cf_y4 ~ 1*cf_y3
    ld_y2 =~ 1*cf_y2
    ld_y3 =~ 1*cf_y3
    ld_y4 =~ 1*cf_y4
    ld_y2 ~ 0*1
    ld_y3 ~ 0*1
    ld_y4 ~ 0*1
    ld_y2 ~~ 0*ld_y2
    ld_y3 ~~ 0*ld_y3
    ld_y4 ~~ 0*ld_y4
    A_y =~ 1*ld_y2
          + 1*ld_y3
          + 1*ld_y4
    A_y ~ 1
    A_y ~~ A_var_y*A_y
    A_y ~~ cf_y1
    ld_y2 ~ ar_y*cf_y1
    ld_y3 ~ ar_y*cf_y2
    ld_y4 ~ ar_y*cf_y3
    ld_x2 ~ start(-0.2)*cl_yx*cf_y1
    ld_y2 ~ start(-0.2)*cl_xy*cf_x1
    ld_x3 ~ start(-0.2)*cl_yx*cf_y2
    ld_y3 ~ start(-0.2)*cl_xy*cf_x2
    ld_x4 ~ start(-0.2)*cl_yx*cf_y3
    ld_y4 ~ start(-0.2)*cl_xy*cf_x3
    A_x ~~ A_y
    cf_x1 ~~ cf_y1
    cf_x1 ~~ A_y
    cf_y1 ~~ A_x




3.2.3 Bivariate LCSM without constant change
Dropping the accumulating factor leaves only proportional change and coupling — the LCSM analogue of the CLPM in change form.

estimateLChange(waves                    = 4,
                variable_type            = "bivariate",
                estimate_constant_change = FALSE) |>
  cat()

    cf_x1 =~ 1*x1
    cf_x2 =~ 1*x2
    cf_x3 =~ 1*x3
    cf_x4 =~ 1*x4
    cf_x1 ~ 1
    cf_x2 ~ 0*1
    cf_x3 ~ 0*1
    cf_x4 ~ 0*1
    cf_x1 ~~ start(15)*cf_x1
    cf_x2 ~~ 0*cf_x2
    cf_x3 ~~ 0*cf_x3
    cf_x4 ~~ 0*cf_x4
    x1 ~ 0*1
    x2 ~ 0*1
    x3 ~ 0*1
    x4 ~ 0*1
    x1 ~~ u_var_x*x1
    x2 ~~ u_var_x*x2
    x3 ~~ u_var_x*x3
    x4 ~~ u_var_x*x4
    cf_x2 ~ 1*cf_x1
    cf_x3 ~ 1*cf_x2
    cf_x4 ~ 1*cf_x3
    ld_x2 =~ 1*cf_x2
    ld_x3 =~ 1*cf_x3
    ld_x4 =~ 1*cf_x4
    ld_x2 ~ 0*1
    ld_x3 ~ 0*1
    ld_x4 ~ 0*1
    ld_x2 ~~ d_var_x*ld_x2
    ld_x3 ~~ d_var_x*ld_x3
    ld_x4 ~~ d_var_x*ld_x4
    ld_x2 ~ start(-0.15)*ar_x*cf_x1
    ld_x3 ~ start(-0.15)*ar_x*cf_x2
    ld_x4 ~ start(-0.15)*ar_x*cf_x3
    cf_y1 =~ 1*y1
    cf_y2 =~ 1*y2
    cf_y3 =~ 1*y3
    cf_y4 =~ 1*y4
    cf_y1 ~ 1
    cf_y2 ~ 0*1
    cf_y3 ~ 0*1
    cf_y4 ~ 0*1
    cf_y1 ~~ start(15)*cf_y1
    cf_y2 ~~ 0*cf_y2
    cf_y3 ~~ 0*cf_y3
    cf_y4 ~~ 0*cf_y4
    y1 ~ 0*1
    y2 ~ 0*1
    y3 ~ 0*1
    y4 ~ 0*1
    y1 ~~ u_var_y*y1
    y2 ~~ u_var_y*y2
    y3 ~~ u_var_y*y3
    y4 ~~ u_var_y*y4
    cf_y2 ~ 1*cf_y1
    cf_y3 ~ 1*cf_y2
    cf_y4 ~ 1*cf_y3
    ld_y2 =~ 1*cf_y2
    ld_y3 =~ 1*cf_y3
    ld_y4 =~ 1*cf_y4
    ld_y2 ~ 0*1
    ld_y3 ~ 0*1
    ld_y4 ~ 0*1
    ld_y2 ~~ d_var_y*ld_y2
    ld_y3 ~~ d_var_y*ld_y3
    ld_y4 ~~ d_var_y*ld_y4
    ld_y2 ~ ar_y*cf_y1
    ld_y3 ~ ar_y*cf_y2
    ld_y4 ~ ar_y*cf_y3
    ld_x2 ~ start(-0.2)*cl_yx*cf_y1
    ld_y2 ~ start(-0.2)*cl_xy*cf_x1
    ld_x3 ~ start(-0.2)*cl_yx*cf_y2
    ld_y3 ~ start(-0.2)*cl_xy*cf_x2
    ld_x4 ~ start(-0.2)*cl_yx*cf_y3
    ld_y4 ~ start(-0.2)*cl_xy*cf_x3
    cf_x1 ~~ cf_y1
    ld_x2 ~~ d_cov_xy*ld_y2
    ld_x3 ~~ d_cov_xy*ld_y3
    ld_x4 ~~ d_cov_xy*ld_y4




3.2.4 Bivariate LCSM with change-to-change effects
Requires \(\geq 4\) waves; adds parameters phi_x, phi_y (change AR) and phi_cl_x, phi_cl_y (change cross-lag).
Unfortunately, there is not a “level” analogue to this specification. The change-to-change parameters capture change on change itself. In particular, \(\phi_x\) is the effect of prior change in \(x\) on subsequent change in \(x\), while \(\phi_{cl_x}\) is the effect of prior change in \(y\) on current/subsequent change in \(x\).

estimateLChange(waves                     = 4,
                constrain_beta           = TRUE,
                constrain_omega          = TRUE,
                estimate_constant_change = TRUE,
                variable_type             = "bivariate",
                estimate_change_to_change = TRUE) |>
  cat()

    cf_x1 =~ 1*x1
    cf_x2 =~ 1*x2
    cf_x3 =~ 1*x3
    cf_x4 =~ 1*x4
    cf_x1 ~ 1
    cf_x2 ~ 0*1
    cf_x3 ~ 0*1
    cf_x4 ~ 0*1
    cf_x1 ~~ start(15)*cf_x1
    cf_x2 ~~ 0*cf_x2
    cf_x3 ~~ 0*cf_x3
    cf_x4 ~~ 0*cf_x4
    x1 ~ 0*1
    x2 ~ 0*1
    x3 ~ 0*1
    x4 ~ 0*1
    x1 ~~ u_var_x*x1
    x2 ~~ u_var_x*x2
    x3 ~~ u_var_x*x3
    x4 ~~ u_var_x*x4
    cf_x2 ~ 1*cf_x1
    cf_x3 ~ 1*cf_x2
    cf_x4 ~ 1*cf_x3
    ld_x2 =~ 1*cf_x2
    ld_x3 =~ 1*cf_x3
    ld_x4 =~ 1*cf_x4
    ld_x2 ~ 0*1
    ld_x3 ~ 0*1
    ld_x4 ~ 0*1
    ld_x2 ~~ 0*ld_x2
    ld_x3 ~~ 0*ld_x3
    ld_x4 ~~ 0*ld_x4
    A_x =~ 1*ld_x2
    + 1*ld_x3
    + 1*ld_x4
    A_x ~ 1
    A_x ~~ A_var_x*A_x
    A_x ~~ cf_x1
    ld_x2 ~ start(-0.15)*ar_x*cf_x1
    ld_x3 ~ start(-0.15)*ar_x*cf_x2
    ld_x4 ~ start(-0.15)*ar_x*cf_x3
    cf_y1 =~ 1*y1
    cf_y2 =~ 1*y2
    cf_y3 =~ 1*y3
    cf_y4 =~ 1*y4
    cf_y1 ~ 1
    cf_y2 ~ 0*1
    cf_y3 ~ 0*1
    cf_y4 ~ 0*1
    cf_y1 ~~ start(15)*cf_y1
    cf_y2 ~~ 0*cf_y2
    cf_y3 ~~ 0*cf_y3
    cf_y4 ~~ 0*cf_y4
    y1 ~ 0*1
    y2 ~ 0*1
    y3 ~ 0*1
    y4 ~ 0*1
    y1 ~~ u_var_y*y1
    y2 ~~ u_var_y*y2
    y3 ~~ u_var_y*y3
    y4 ~~ u_var_y*y4
    cf_y2 ~ 1*cf_y1
    cf_y3 ~ 1*cf_y2
    cf_y4 ~ 1*cf_y3
    ld_y2 =~ 1*cf_y2
    ld_y3 =~ 1*cf_y3
    ld_y4 =~ 1*cf_y4
    ld_y2 ~ 0*1
    ld_y3 ~ 0*1
    ld_y4 ~ 0*1

          + 1*ld_y3

title: 3  Latent Change Score Models
text:
3.3 simLChange(): Simulating from the LCSM
simLChange() simulates data under user specified true values. Arguments mirror the estimator syntax: beta_x/beta_y (proportional change), omega_x/omega_y (coupling), constant_mean_x/constant_mean_y and latent_variance_x/latent_variance_y (constant-change factor mean and variance), and estimate_constant_change to toggle accumulating change.

sim <- simLChange(
  waves                    = 5,
  variable_type            = "bivariate",
  beta_x                   = -0.7,
  beta_y                   = -0.7,
  omega_x                  = 0.5,
  omega_y                  = 0.5,
  estimate_constant_change = FALSE,
  sample.nobs              = 500
)
str(sim$data, list.len = 6)

title: 3  Latent Change Score Models
section: 3.4 Algebraic Equivalence: LCSM = CLPM in Change Form
text:
3.4 Algebraic Equivalence: LCSM = CLPM in Change Form
Without a constant-change factor and without measurement error, the LCSM dynamic equation \(\Delta x_t = \beta_x x_{t-1} + \omega_x y_{t-1} + u\) rearranges to \(x_t = (1+\beta_x) x_{t-1} + \omega_x y_{t-1} + u\) — exactly the CLPM regression on the level scores. The CLPM should recover \(1+\beta\) for autoregressives and \(\omega\) for cross-lags. I do a lot of this throughout the document – it’s useful to see the equivalence across models

Measurement error. simLChange() defaults indicator_variance_x = indicator_variance_y = 0.5, putting noise on the observed scores. The CLPM has no latent measurement model, so its coefficients are attenuated by errors-in-variables. The LCSM separates true scores from indicators and is immune.
Constant change. When the accumulating factor is on, each unit carries a stable trajectory; the CLPM treats this as wave-specific variation and biases AR up / CL down.

The simulated data – under the latent change specification – leads to estimates for the CLPM which mirror the generating parameters.

clean <- simLChange(
  waves                    = 5,
  variable_type            = "bivariate",
  beta_x                   = -0.2,
  beta_y                   = -0.2,
  omega_x                  = 0.20,
  omega_y                  = 0.55,
  indicator_variance_x     = 0.001,
  indicator_variance_y     = 0.001,
  estimate_constant_change = FALSE,
  sample.nobs              = 1000
)
cat("DGP: LCSM, no constant change, near-zero measurement error.\n",
    "Expect AR_x ≈ AR_y ≈ 0.80, CL_yx ≈ 0.20, CL_xy ≈ 0.55.\n", sep = "")

title: 3  Latent Change Score Models
text:
3.6 Summary
The LCSM is a powerful framework for modeling dynamics in change form. By differencing observed scores, it effectively absorbs all time-invariant heterogeneity, including stable between-person differences. The estimateLChange() function generates lavaan syntax for a variety of LCSM specifications, while simLChange() simulates data.
There is a clear algebraic equivalence between the LCSM without constant change and the CLPM in change form. However, the presence of measurement error and/or constant change leads to bias in the CLPM estimates, while the LCSM can recover the true parameters.

title: 4  The Random-Intercept CLPM
text:
4.1 Decomposition
Chapter 1 showed that the CLPM conflates stable between-person differences with within-person dynamics. Chapter 2 addressed this through change scores; the Latent Change Score Model. This chapter takes up the alternative decomposition popularized by Hamaker, Kuiper, and Grasman (2015): the Random-Intercept Cross-Lagged Panel Model (RI-CLPM), which splits each observed score into a stable trait (\(I_x\), \(I_y\)) and a wave-specific within-person deviation (\(p_t\), \(q_t\)). Lagged regressions are then specified on the deviations, not on the raw scores.
\(x_{it}\) and \(y_{it}\) denote the observed scores for person \(i\) at time \(t\). It’s useful to decompose the observed scores into constituent parts: an overall mean for each wave (\(\mu_{xt}, \mu_{yt}\)), a stable trait for each person (\(I_{x,i}, I_{y,i}\)), and a time-varying deviation from that trait (\(x^*_{it}, y^*_{it}\)). Or more compactly, the effect of time, the effect of person, and the effect of random noise around these two components. \[
\begin{aligned}
x_{it} &= \mu_{xt} + I_{x,i} + x^*_{it}, \\
y_{it} &= \mu_{yt} + I_{y,i} + y^*_{it},
\end{aligned}
\]
Unlike the CLPM though, the dynamic equation is not specified on the observed scores, but rather, the deviations from the trait, \(x^*_{it}\) and \(y^*_{it}\). \[
\begin{aligned}
x^*_{it} &= \text{ar}_x\,x^*_{i,t-1} + \text{cl}_{yx}\,y^*_{i,t-1} + u_{x,it}, \\
y^*_{it} &= \text{ar}_y\,y^*_{i,t-1} + \text{cl}_{xy}\,x^*_{i,t-1} + u_{y,it}.
\end{aligned}
\]
ri <- simRICLPM(waves       = 5,
                beta_x      = 0.3,
                beta_y      = 0.3,
                omega_xy    = 0.2,
                omega_yx    = 0.1,
                sample.nobs = 1000,
                var_BX      = 0.5,
                var_BY      = 0.5,
                var_p       = 1,
                var_q       = 1,
                estimate_means = TRUE)

cat("DGP: RI-CLPM | Estimator: CLPM (omits trait factors)\n")

title: 4  The Random-Intercept CLPM
text:
NotecrossLagR syntax: estimateRICLPM()



estimateRICLPM() emits lavaan syntax with the unified labels ar_x, ar_y (within-person carry-over), cl_xy, cl_yx (within-person cross-lags), I_x, I_y (stable traits), and d_var_*, d_cov_xy (dynamic residuals). Optional include_z = TRUE adds a third process; time_invariant_vars regresses traits on covariates.

estimateRICLPM(waves           = 3,
               constrain_beta  = TRUE,
               constrain_omega = TRUE) |>
  cat()


# Random Intercepts (Trait Factors I)
I_x =~ 1*x1 + 1*x2 + 1*x3
I_y =~ 1*y1 + 1*y2 + 1*y3

# Within-Person Latent Variables
p1 =~ 1*x1
q1 =~ 1*y1
p2 =~ 1*x2
q2 =~ 1*y2
p3 =~ 1*x3
q3 =~ 1*y3

# Within-person factor means (fixed to 0)
p1 ~ 0*1
q1 ~ 0*1
p2 ~ 0*1
q2 ~ 0*1
p3 ~ 0*1
q3 ~ 0*1

# Trait factor means (free)
I_x ~ 1
I_y ~ 1

# Observed intercepts (fixed to 0)
x1 ~ 0*1
y1 ~ 0*1
x2 ~ 0*1
y2 ~ 0*1
x3 ~ 0*1
y3 ~ 0*1

# Autoregressive and Cross-lagged Effects
p2 ~ ar_x*p1 + cl_yx*q1
q2 ~ ar_y*q1 + cl_xy*p1
p3 ~ ar_x*p2 + cl_yx*q2
q3 ~ ar_y*q2 + cl_xy*p2

# Dynamic Residual Variances
p1 ~~ d_var_x*p1
q1 ~~ d_var_y*q1
p2 ~~ d_var_x*p2
q2 ~~ d_var_y*q2
p3 ~~ d_var_x*p3
q3 ~~ d_var_y*q3

# Dynamic Residual Covariances
p1 ~~ d_cov_xy*q1
p2 ~~ d_cov_xy*q2
p3 ~~ d_cov_xy*q3

# Trait Factor (I) Variances and Covariances
I_x ~~ I_x
I_y ~~ I_y
I_x ~~ I_y

# Fix Observed Variable Residuals to Zero
x1 ~~ 0*x1
y1 ~~ 0*y1
x2 ~~ 0*x2
y2 ~~ 0*y2
x3 ~~ 0*x3
y3 ~~ 0*y3










NotecrossLagR syntax: simRICLPM()



simRICLPM() simulates from the RI-CLPM at user-specified true values. var_BX, var_BY set the trait variances, var_p, var_q the within-person dynamic-residual variances, and beta_x/beta_y, omega_xy/omega_yx the AR and CL parameters.

sim <- simRICLPM(waves       = 5,
                 beta_x      = 0.3,
                 beta_y      = 0.3,
                 omega_xy    = 0.2,
                 omega_yx    = 0.1,
                 var_BX      = 1.0,
                 var_BY      = 1.0,
                 var_p       = 1.0,
                 var_q       = 1.0,
                 sample.nobs = 1000)

title: 5  Extensions and Alternatives
text:
5.1 The Unified Framework
The CLPM (Chapter 1), the LCSM (Chapter 2), and the RI-CLPM (Chapter 3) are special cases of a broader unified model, which Usami, Murayama, and Hamaker (2019) organizes into two equations: a decomposition component. This is just the way that we can split the observed scores into stable and time-varying components. For instance, the RI-CLPM separates the stable trait from the time-varying deviations around that stable trait. The dynamic equation is typically what one is interested in with a panel dataset, a change process. In the RI-CLPM this is represented by the autoregressive and cross-lagged paths on the time-varying deviations. In the LCM, the dynamic equation is the proportional change specified by the latent change scores, what proportion of y changes for every unit change in x. Usami, Murayama, and Hamaker (2019) shows that many models share common decomposition and dynamic equations, and these two components can be combined and modified to yield different families of models. This can be a useful exercise – there are a number of heavily used structural equation models that can be estimated with panel data, and it isn’t always clear how the parameters of models related to one another. One commonly estimated model is the Latent Growth Model (LGM), which capture each unit’s trajectory over time with a latent intercept and slope factor. The RI-CLPM is a special case of the LGM, where the stable factors are random intercepts and there are no slope factors and dynamic paths are specified on the time-varying deviations from those random intercepts. The crossLagR includes syntax and simulation functions for all the models in this chapter.
Usami, Murayama, and Hamaker (2019) proposes couching these various models in an overarching framework. The notion is to start by acknowledging that observed scores are a combination of stable and time-varying components. This chapter implements this framework to develop several related SEM estimators for panel data, including the Latent Growth Model (LCM), Autoregressive Latent Trajectory (ALT) model, and the General Cross-Lagged Model (GCLM; also known as the Latent Growth Model with Structured Residuals). Under this framework, these models can also be compared to the CLPM, RI-CLPM, and LCSM, which are special cases of the more general framework.
\[
\begin{aligned}
x_{it} &= \mu_{xt} + \underbrace{F_{x,i}^\top \lambda_{xt}}_{\text{stable}} + x^*_{it}, \\
y_{it} &= \mu_{yt} + \underbrace{F_{y,i}^\top \lambda_{yt}}_{\text{stable}} + y^*_{it}.
\end{aligned}
\]
Here \(F_{x,i}, F_{y,i}\) captures an array of stable factors – these wouldn’t specify all at once, but the framework simply allows us to capture an array of things that show up in panel models, that all fall in this bucket: stable components. These are “random intercepts” in the RI-CLPM. They are “growth factors” in the ALT and LCM-SR; they represent the unit specific slope and intercept that capture each unit’s trajectory. These are “latent fixed effects” in Bollen & Brand. Importantly, the loadings \(\lambda_{xt}, \lambda_{yt}\) define how these stable factors contribute to the observed scores at each wave.
The stable component is important in decomposition. It allows us to partition the observed scores into stable and time-varying components. The time-varying within-person component is \(x^*_{it}, y^*_{it}\). \[
\begin{aligned}
x^*_{it} &= \beta_x\,x^*_{i,t-1} + \omega_{yx}\,y^*_{i,t-1} + u_{x,it}, \\
y^*_{it} &= \beta_y\,y^*_{i,t-1} + \omega_{xy}\,x^*_{i,t-1} + u_{y,it}.
\end{aligned}
\]
where \(\beta_x, \beta_y\) are the autoregressive coefficients (lavaan labels ar_x, ar_y) and \(\omega_{xy}, \omega_{yx}\) are the cross-lagged coefficients (lavaan labels cl_xy, cl_yx).1
Each estimator in crossLagR largely depend on the stable factor component. Let’s say that any model is defined by three broad categories of parameters: the stable factors \(F\), their loadings \(\lambda_t\), and the dynamic equation (what goes on in the time-varying component).
The crossLagR package uses identical parameter labels across models, but the interpretation of these parameters differs by model. ?tbl-unified-labels-short summarizes the unified parameter labels across models, their varying substantive interpretations.
?tbl-unified-labels-short briefly summarizes this varying interpretation. The CLPM, for instance, does not have a stable trait component, so the AR paths capture rank-order stability (if an individual scores higher than their own average at time 1, they also score higher than their own average at time 2?). The model generally will preserve the rank-order of individuals over time, in \(x\) and \(y\).
: Parameter interpretations by model. {#tbl-unified-labels-short}

title: 5  Extensions and Alternatives
text:
What stable factors \(F\)? In the CLPM, there are no stable factors – all variation is wave-specific. In the RI-CLPM, there are two stable factors – the random intercepts for x and y. In the ALT and LCM-SR, there are four stable factors – the intercept and slope for x and y. In Bollen & Brand, there are two stable factors – the latent fixed effects for x and y.
The trait factors are expressed by the loadings \(\lambda_t\) This is particularly important in growth models, where the loadings determine how the stable factors contribute to the observed scores at each wave, for instance.
Dynamic Effects In the RI-CLPM the dynamic equations are estimated on \(x^*\) or directly. In the Autoregressive Latent Trajectory (ALT) model, the AR and CL paths are specified directly the observed scores, which creates a compounding effect over time. And General Cross Cross-Lagged Model which is a growth model, where the residuals are then regressed in a cross lag structure. So, unlike the RI-CLPM the GCLM autoregressive and cross-lagged paths are deviations from a unit’s growth trajectory, not deviations from a unit’s trait.

title: 5  Extensions and Alternatives
text:
5.2 The Latent Growth Model
The LGM is a different style of model, in that each unit has an intercept \(I\) (initial level) and slope \(S\) (linear rate of change).In the bivariate form, \((I_x, S_x)\) and \((I_y, S_y)\) covary across variables but no lagged paths connect \(x\) to \(y\). This is the natural baseline for the ALT and LCM-SR, both of which add a dynamic equation on top of an LGM-style stable component.








Figure 5.1: Bivariate Latent Growth Model. Growth factors (I, S) load directly on observed scores with fixed loadings (intercept = 1; slope = 0, 1, 2, 3); no autoregressive or cross-lagged paths.










NotecrossLagR syntax: estimateLGM()




estimateLGM(waves = 4, variable_type = "bivariate") |> cat()

# Latent Variables
p1 =~ 1*x1
q1 =~ 1*y1
p2 =~ 1*x2
q2 =~ 1*y2
p3 =~ 1*x3
q3 =~ 1*y3
p4 =~ 1*x4
q4 =~ 1*y4

# Latent Growth Factors for X
I_x =~ 1*p1 + 1*p2 + 1*p3 + 1*p4
S_x =~ 0*p1 + 1*p2 + 2*p3 + 3*p4

# Latent Growth Factors for Y
I_y =~ 1*q1 + 1*q2 + 1*q3 + 1*q4
S_y =~ 0*q1 + 1*q2 + 2*q3 + 3*q4

# Growth Factor Means
I_x ~ mean_I_x*1
S_x ~ mean_S_x*1
I_y ~ mean_I_y*1
S_y ~ mean_S_y*1

# Growth Factor Variances
I_x ~~ var_I_x*I_x
S_x ~~ var_S_x*S_x
I_y ~~ var_I_y*I_y
S_y ~~ var_S_y*S_y

# Within-Variable Growth Factor Covariances
I_x ~~ cov_IS_x*S_x

# Between-Variable Growth Factor Covariances
I_x ~~ cov_I_xy*I_y
S_x ~~ cov_S_xy*S_y
I_x ~~ cov_IS_xy*S_y
I_y ~~ cov_IS_yx*S_x

# Latent Variable Means (Fixed to 0)
p1 ~ 0*1
q1 ~ 0*1
p2 ~ 0*1
q2 ~ 0*1
p3 ~ 0*1
q3 ~ 0*1

q4 ~ 0*1

# Observed Variable Intercepts (Fixed to 0)

y3 ~ 0*1

title: 5  Extensions and Alternatives
text:
5.3 ALT: Autoregressive Latent Trajectory
The ALT is a combination of the LGM and the CLPM. Each wave score (x and y) is a combination of a growth trajectory (as in the LGM) and a dynamic process (as in the CLPM). The problem with the ALT is that the trajectories and dynamics are not cleanly separated, and the growth factors compound with the AR and CL paths (Usami (2021), (usami2022?)). The AR and CL parameters may thus be different from the CLPM or RI-CLPM.








Figure 5.2: Bivariate ALT. Growth factors (I, S) load on observed scores; AR (β) and CL (ω) operate directly on the scores, so growth and dynamics compound.

            constrain_beta  = TRUE,
            constrain_omega = TRUE) |>

title: 5  Extensions and Alternatives
text:
5.4 GCLM
The General Cross-Lagged Model (GCLM; also known as the Latent Growth Model with Structured Residuals) is similar to the ALT, in that it combines a growth model with a dynamic equation. However, in the GCLM, the growth factors have direct effects on the observed scores, and the AR and CL paths are specified on the residuals from that growth model. The AR and CL parameters capture the dynamic process in the deviations from each person’s trajectory, rather than deviations from a stable trait (as in the RI-CLPM).








Figure 5.3: Bivariate LCM-SR / GCLM. Growth factors (I, S) have direct effects only on observed scores; AR (β) and CL (ω) act on the structured residuals (q, p) — deviations from each person’s trajectory.

title: 5  Extensions and Alternatives
section: 5.5 Bollen & Brand: Dynamic Panel with Latent Fixed Effects
text:
5.5 Bollen & Brand: Dynamic Panel with Latent Fixed Effects
Bollen and Brand (2010) propose a dynamic panel model in which a latent factor absorbs the unit-specific fixed effect — it forms an SEM analogue of Arellano-Bond (see also Dishop and DeShon 2018, the practical tutorial implementation that estimateBollen_and_Brand() follows).
The decomposition equation is important. In the RI-CLPM, the stable factor is a random intercept. Each unit is drawn from a distribution of units. The model captures unobserved heterogeneity across units. But a critical identifying assumption in the RI-CLPM is that the stable factors is uncorrelated with the time varying components. If this is violated, the RI-CLPM estimates are biased.
A useful alternative — and one with the same spirit as the well-known Arellano-Bond GMM estimator, though here estimated by maximum likelihood (Bollen and Brand 2010; Dishop and DeShon 2018) — is to specify the stable factor as a latent unit-effect that loads on every wave of the observed CLPM equation, while freely estimating its covariance with the lagged predictors. It’s this part that is critical: Letting that covariance be non-zero is the relaxes the orthogonality assumption that the RI-CLPM imposes, and it is exactly the source of the dynamic-panel bias identified by Nickell (1981) clearly elaborated upon in influential texbooks ((Hsiao 2022)) And Lüdtke and Robitzsch (2022) show empirically that this matters — the Bollen-Brand specification and the RI-CLPM can yield different cross-lag estimates on the same data because of the departures in how the dynamic equation is specified.
The crossLagR function estimateBollen_and_Brand() implements the specification. Including all the covariances.






NotecrossLagR syntax: estimateBollen_and_Brand()




estimateBollen_and_Brand(waves = 4) |> cat()

# Latent fixed effects (individual heterogeneity, accumulating factors)
I_y =~ 1*y2 + 1*y3 + 1*y4
I_x =~ 1*x2 + 1*x3 + 1*x4

# Regression equations
y2 ~ ar_y2*y1 + cl_xy2*x1
y3 ~ ar_y3*y2 + cl_xy3*x2
y4 ~ ar_y4*y3 + cl_xy4*x3
x2 ~ ar_x2*x1 + cl_yx2*y1
x3 ~ ar_x3*x2 + cl_yx3*y2
x4 ~ ar_x4*x3 + cl_yx4*y3

# Exogenous variable variances
y1 ~~ y1
x1 ~~ x1

# Exogenous variable covariances
y1 ~~ x1

# Latent fixed effect (I) variances and covariances
I_y ~~ I_y
I_x ~~ I_x
I_y ~~ I_x

# Exogenous-I covariances
y1 ~~ I_y
y1 ~~ I_x
x1 ~~ I_y
x1 ~~ I_x

# Dynamic residual variances
y2 ~~ d_var_y*y2
y3 ~~ d_var_y*y3
y4 ~~ d_var_y*y4
x2 ~~ d_var_x*x2
x3 ~~ d_var_x*x3
x4 ~~ d_var_x*x4

title: 5  Extensions and Alternatives
text:
5.6 Comparing on One Dataset
Fitting several estimators to one dataset is mechanical because the lavaan labels are unified: ar_x, ar_y, cl_xy, cl_yx mean the AR/CL parameters of that particular model and can be pulled directly from parameterEstimates(). The problem with this – of course, is that the estimates are not directly comparable across models, because they have different interpretations.
Let’s simulate data from a RI-CLPM DGP, and fit all the models to it. So, RI-CLPM is will fit well, and the truth table is:

It’s useful to see the model similarities, but it’s also important to interpret the estimates with caution. The AR coefficient means carry-over on observed scores in the CLPM, carry-over on within-person deviations in the RI-CLPM, proportional change in the LCSM (effective AR is \(1 + \beta_x\)), compounding carry-over in the ALT, carry-over in residuals from growth in the LCM-SR, and carry-over net of a latent fixed effect in Bollen & Brand.

title: 5  Extensions and Alternatives
text:
5.7 Summary
Usami, Murayama, and Hamaker (2019) introduce a useful framework for understanding the relationships between different panel data models. The framework follows from from two key equations, the decomposition equation of observed scores into stable and time-varying components, and the dynamic equation that captures how the time-varying components evolve over time. All models in this chapter can be expressed in the same framework, the differences between them are in how the stable component is defined, and how the dynamic equation is specified – e.g,, on the observed scores, or deviations from a stable trait or growth trajectory.




Bollen, Kenneth A., and Jennie E. Brand. 2010. “A General Panel Model with Random and Fixed Effects: A Structural Equations Approach.” Social Forces 89 (1): 1–34.


Dishop, Christopher R., and Richard P. DeShon. 2018. “A Tutorial on Bollen and Brand’s Approach to Modeling Dynamics Using a System of Regression Equations.” Psychological Methods 23 (4): 1089–1112.


Hsiao, Cheng. 2022. Analysis of Panel Data. 64. Cambridge university press.


Lüdtke, Oliver, and Alexander Robitzsch. 2022. “A Comparison of Different Approaches for Estimating Cross-Lagged Effects from a Causal Inference Perspective.” Structural Equation Modeling: A Multidisciplinary Journal 29 (6): 888–907.


Usami, Satoshi. 2021. “On the Differences Between General Cross-Lagged Panel Model and Random-Intercept Cross-Lagged Panel Model: Interpretation of Cross-Lagged Parameters and Model Choice.” Structural Equation Modeling: A Multidisciplinary Journal 28 (3): 331–44.


Usami, Satoshi, Kou Murayama, and Ellen L. Hamaker. 2019. “A Unified Framework of Longitudinal Models to Examine Reciprocal Relations.” Psychological Methods 24 (5): 637–57. https://doi.org/10.1037/met0000210.

title: 5  Extensions and Alternatives
text: In the estimateRICLPM() lavaan syntax, the within-person deviations \(x^*_{it}\) and \(y^*_{it}\) are written as latent variables p and q, respectively (i.e., p1, p2, ... correspond to \(x^*_{i,1}, x^*_{i,2}, \ldots\) and q1, q2, ... to \(y^*_{i,1}, y^*_{i,2}, \ldots\)). The dynamic equation above is therefore implemented as p ~ ar_x*p + cl_yx*q and q ~ ar_y*q + cl_xy*p.↩︎

title: 6  Simulations
text:
6.1 Population Models
The previous chapters established that the CLPM, RI-CLPM, LCSM, ALT, LCM-SR, and Bollen & Brand all share the same four AR/CL labels but differ in how they decompose observed variance. The substantive question is empirical: when each model is fit to data that was not generated under its own assumptions, what does it recover?
This chapter answers that question with a focused simulation experiment.
Simulation — Heterogeneity. Data are generated from an RI-CLPM and the between-to-within variance ratio is varied from 0.25 (mostly within-person noise) to 9 (mostly stable trait). All six estimators are fit to each replication.
All AR/CL parameters in the DGP are held at the same target values, so the only thing changing across conditions is the amount of between-person heterogeneity.
Each DGP is a population lavaan parameter table built by piping the estimator’s syntax through lavaanify(), then overwriting the variance components.

title: 6  Simulations
text: 6.2 Estimators and Fit Helper

title: 6  Simulations
text:
6.3 Simulation 1: Varying Between-Person Heterogeneity
Data are generated from the RI-CLPM at each bw_ratio \(\in \{0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 9\}\). Each cell is replicated 100 times. All six estimators are fit to each replication.

6.3.1 Distributions of Estimates
Figure 6.1 shows the sampling distribution of each estimator’s recovery of the four AR/CL parameters. Vertical lines mark the true value; each ridge is colored by its mean absolute deviation from truth — the more green the closer to the truth, red ridges drift far from the true value. One important caveat: The LCGM autoregressive parameters drift far from true parameter values, but the AR parameter should be interpreted as \(1+\beta\) where \(\beta\) is the true slope generated from the RI-CLPM.








Figure 6.1: Sampling distribution of AR/CL estimates under the RI-CLPM DGP, by between:within variance ratio. Columns are parameters, rows are estimators. The vertical dashed line marks the true value. Fill encodes mean |estimate − truth| for that cell: green = on target, red = far from truth.







6.3.2 Bias and Precision
Figure 6.2 collapses the distributions into mean bias with 95% MC intervals. It’s also pretty revealing. The CLPM breaks down with a moderate amount of trait variance. Also the spikes that appear for some are because only a handful of models actually converge, so these are means of only a handful of actual estimates.








Figure 6.2: Bias of AR/CL estimates as a function of between:within variance ratio (RI-CLPM DGP). Points are means across replications; vertical segments are 95% Monte Carlo intervals. The dashed line at zero is unbiased.





The RMSEA estimates vary wildly across models Figure 6.3, and the CLPM generally fits poorly. Though most models collapse when there is effectively only trait variance.
Figure 6.3








Figure 6.3: Root mean squared error of AR/CL estimates under the RI-CLPM DGP, by between:within variance ratio. Lower is better. The RI-CLPM line should stay flat; misspecified estimators should climb as the trait variance grows.







6.3.3 Convergence
Table 6.2 displays the convergence statistics. This is an important indicator of each models ability to operate in extreme conditions, but conditions that are quite common in panel data in the social sciences. The convergence rate is simply the proportion of replications that produce parameter estimates. The Heywood rate is the proportion of converged replications that produce at least one negative variance estimate. These errors are quite common in over parameterized models. The ALT and LCM-SR have very high rates of Heywood cases, especially as the trait variance grows.

One of the issues with the RI-CLPM is that it can be difficult to fit when there is a lot of trait variance. The Bayesian approach allows us to put informative priors on the parameters.

text:
7.5 The Intraclass Correlation Coefficient (ICC)
The Analysis of Variance model simply decomposes the total variance for a variable into within and between components. The ICC (\(\rho\)) is the share of total variance attributable to stable between-person differences. We can easily calculate this on the long data using a multilevel model in lme4
\[
    ext{ICC} \;=\; \frac{\sigma^2_{\text{between}}}{\sigma^2_{\text{between}} + \sigma^2_{\text{within}}}
\]

7.6 Validation with the TSO
Another way to estimate these component is with a TSO model.


# Trait factors (between-person)
T_x =~ 1*x1 + 1*x2 + 1*x3 + 1*x4
T_y =~ 1*y1 + 1*y2 + 1*y3 + 1*y4

# Trait variances and covariance
T_x ~~ T_var_x*T_x
T_y ~~ T_var_y*T_y
T_x ~~ T_cov_xy*T_y

# State variances (within-person)
x1 ~~ s_var_x*x1
y1 ~~ s_var_y*y1
x2 ~~ s_var_x*x2
y2 ~~ s_var_y*y2
x3 ~~ s_var_x*x3
y3 ~~ s_var_y*y3
x4 ~~ s_var_x*x4
y4 ~~ s_var_y*y4

# Within-time state covariances
x1 ~~ s_cov_xy*y1
x2 ~~ s_cov_xy*y2
x3 ~~ s_cov_xy*y3
x4 ~~ s_cov_xy*y4

title: 7  The ISCAP Panel
text:
7.7 Arellano-Bond Dynamic Panel Model
The Arelleno-Bond estimator uses GMM on the first-differenced data – to remove the unit effects – with lagged variables serving as instruments for the lagged right-hand side differenced variables.
Now let’s fit:

Political legitimacy as the outcome and party identification as the predictor
Party identification as the outcome and political legitimacy as the predictor. Both models include a lag of the dependent variable on the right-hand side to capture autoregression. The instrument set includes lags 2 and 3 of the dependent variable, and lag 1 of the predictor variable. This is a strong assumption for party id – typically the Arellano-Bond instrument matrix unfolds such that wave 1 of the predictor is used as an instrument for the change from wave 2 to wave 3, wave 1 and 2 are used to instrument the change from wave 2 to 3, and wave 1, 2, and 3 are used to instrument the change from wave 3 to 4. Party ID is so stable that it is exogenous. The results show a strong autoregressive effect for both variables, but no evidence of cross-lagged effects.



Oneway (individual) effect Two-steps model Difference GMM

    1) + lag(party_identification, 1) | lag(political_legitimacy, 
    2:3) | lag(party_identification, 1), data = ab_data, effect = "individual", 
    model = "twosteps", transformation = "d")

The model is useful, but it does assume something that is problematic in this context: The correlation between the lagged IV and the DV should change from wave to wave; in this case, from election-to-election, with presidents of both political parties. If a Democrat is in office, Republicans should become more critical; if a Republican is in office, Democrats should be more critical.
A similar model, estimated via Maximum Likelihood versus Generalized Method of Moments, is the Bollen and Brand dynamic panel model, which is described in the next section.

title: 7  The ISCAP Panel
section: 7.8 Bollen and Brand Dynamic Panel Model
text:
7.8 Bollen and Brand Dynamic Panel Model
The Bollen and Brand (2010) dynamic panel model captures autoregression, cross-lagged effects, and unobserved heterogeneity (latent fixed effects) within an SEM framework (see also Dishop and DeShon 2018). The diagram below shows the reciprocal cross-lag specification with 4 waves.






Because we freely estimate the AR and CL parameters, there are far more statistically significant coefficients – they just change signs on occasion, which is why they were, on average, non-significant in the Arellano-Bond model. And lagged legitimacy never predicts PID – which makes sense, because PID is stable, but its correlation with legitimacy is not.

The crossLagR package includes functions to generate lavaan syntax for the Cross-Lagged Panel Model (CLPM), the Random Intercept Cross-Lagged Panel Model (RICLPM), and the Allison-Chamberlain Fixed Effects model. An alternative way to do this is to specify a random intercept for both wave and identifier, with random coefficients for partisanship and wave. The way it is currently specified is the wave variable is fixed, all observations pull around a common value. This now captures heterogeneity in the alignment of partisanship and legitimacy over time.
\[\begin{eqnarray}
y_{it}=b_{0,i}+b_{1,i} x_{it}+ \sum_{t=2012}^{2020}\omega_{1,t} d_{t} + \sum_{t=2012}^{2020}\omega_{2,t} d_{t} x_{it} + e_{it}\\
b_{0,i} \sim N(b_0, \sigma_0^2)\\
b_{1,i} \sim N(b_1, \sigma_1^2) \\
\omega_{1,t} \sim N (\omega_1, \sigma_{\omega_1}^2) \\
\omega_{2,t} \sim N (\omega_2, \sigma_{\omega_2}^2) \\
\end{eqnarray}\]
When the Democratic candidate wins, Democrats are more likely to view the political system as legitimate, and when the Republican candidate wins, Republicans are more likely to view the political system as legitimate.

7.12.1 Expertise, Partisanship, and Political Legitimacy

title: 8  Replication: Bakker, Lelkes, & Malka (2021)
text:
8.1 Setup
This chapter relies on the LISS panel analysis from Bakker, Lelkes, and Malka (2021), using crossLagR::estimateCLPM(). Three waves (May 2008, May 2013, May 2019 for personality; Dec 2007–2018 for politics); “closed personality” = openness (reversed) + conscientiousness; and five political items were analyzed, consistent with the models found in Bakker, Lelkes, and Malka (2021). So, this forms a three-wave panel with two observed variables (closed personality and each political item), and we fit a separate CLPM for each political item.
This chapter forms a partial replication of Bakker, Lelkes, and Malka (2021) LISS panel, re-estimates their models with alternative models in crossLagR, and checks the factor structure of the personality items and each Big Five trait’s effects on political preferences, and vice versa, across various specifications. However, the chapter is intended to address a broader topic in political psychology, the relationship between personality and political preferences, and which construct is causally prior.

title: 8  Replication: Bakker, Lelkes, & Malka (2021)
text:
8.2 Reliability (Cronbach’s \(\alpha\))
Here, we identify the LISS personality and political variables and calculate Cronbach’s alpha for each scale.
There are so many variables used in the analysis (with just one x and y) that it’s easier to list them together in a named list, and then just loop through all the names in the list to calculate the alphas. We follow the same approach throughout the analysis.


# A tibble: 8 × 4
  scale             n_items n_obs alpha
  <chr>               <int> <int> <dbl>
1 Openness               10 72343 0.766
2 Conscientiousness      10 72343 0.785
3 Extraversion           10 72343 0.876
4 Agreeableness          10 72343 0.818
5 Neuroticism            10 72343 0.89 
6 PANAS_Positive         10 71887 0.877
7 PANAS_Negative         10 71887 0.932
8 Politics                9 74137 0.76 


(alpha-table?) shows that the scales have acceptable reliability.

title: 8  Replication: Bakker, Lelkes, & Malka (2021)
text:
8.3 Closed Personality
Bakker, Lelkes, and Malka (2021) estimate a CLPM with conscientiousness and openness scales as indicators of a latent “closed personality” factor. These constructs independently (but equally) are included in the regression model, but they constrain “incoming” and “outgoing” paths to be equal across the two constructs. They also account for the fact that the data are in long format (subject x personality construct x wave) and adjust the standard errors accordingly.
We took a somewhat different route by constructing a “closed personality” composite – but do analyze each personality construct separately later this in this chapter. We simply combine the conscientiousness and openness items into a respondent composite score. Aggregating and then disaggregating “closed personality” into its two Big 5 components allows us to check whether the results are driven by one trait or the other. This also sidesteps the issue of whether the two traits should be specified to have equal paths in the CLPM, which is a rather strong assumption. Finally, the crossLagR package, which builds models in lavaan, is designed (or at least biased towards) data that are in wide format (one row per subject, with separate columns for each wave and variable), which makes averaging (or specifying a latent variable) advantageous.
The LISS data we analyze consists of three waves, 2008, 2013, 2019 and a separate CLPM for each of five political items: left–right self-placement, euthanasia, income differences, European integration, immigration.

8.3.1 CLPM Results
We leave the autoregressive and cross-lagged paths unconstrained across waves (so wave-2-on-wave-1 coefficients differ from wave-3-on-wave-2).
We also standardize the variables.
We plot our estimates in ?fig-bakker, which closely replicates the main figure from Bakker, Lelkes, and Malka (2021). Again, we suspect the slight deviations are an artifact of how the personality variables modeled in the CLPM.

title: 8  Replication: Bakker, Lelkes, & Malka (2021)
text:
8.4 The Intra Class Correlation
The intraclass correlation \(\text{ICC} = \sigma^2_b / (\sigma^2_b + \sigma^2_w)\) gives the share of total variance attributable to stable between-person differences. We estimate it with an empty random-intercept model via lmer: y ~ 1 + (1 | case_id). High ICC means most variance is between-person (trait-like); this is the variance that biases the CLPM cross-lagged coefficients in the next section.


# A tibble: 2 × 2
  variable             ICC
  <chr>              <dbl>
1 Closed Personality 0.77 
2 Politics Scale     0.665


Both politics and personality are reasonably stable – but the ICC is still far from 1.

title: 8  Replication: Bakker, Lelkes, & Malka (2021)
text:
8.6 Accounting for Unit Effects
The CLPM coefficients above leave one obvious question: would Bakker’s substantive conclusion survive an estimator that actually partitions between- from within-person variance? The CLPM treats every wave of variation as wave-specific. With the closed-personality composite ICC at ~0.6 (next section), that’s almost certainly mis-attributing trait variance to autoregression and attenuating cross-lags. We refit the same data with four alternatives from the Usami, Murayama, and Hamaker (2019) unified family — RI-CLPM, Latent Change Score, Bollen & Brand, and GCLM.

8.6.1 RI-CLPM
The RI-CLPM extracts a stable trait component for both closed and the politics item, then estimates AR/CL on the within-person deviations from those traits.


8.6.2 Latent Change Score Model
The LCSM rewrites the dynamic in terms of wave-to-wave changes rather than levels, with an accumulating constant-change factor to absorb stable trajectories – heterogeneity in the change process. The cross-lag interpretation flips: cl_xy is now level of politics at \(t-1\) predicts the change in closed personality between \(t-1\) and \(t\), for instance.


8.6.3 Bollen-Brand CLPM
Bollen & Brand absorb unit-specific heterogeneity into a latent fixed effect — the SEM analogue of Arellano-Bond — and condition on the first wave as exogenous. The RI-CLPM assumes indepedence between the trait and state components. The Bollen-Brand model includes covariances between the latent fixed effect and the observed variables at each wave, which allows for correlation between the trait and state components.


8.6.4 LCM-SR
The Latent Curve Model with Structured Residuals (also called the GCLM) fits a bivariate latent growth model and then estimates AR/CL dynamics on the residuals from each person’s trajectory.
Figure 8.2 and Table 8.3 provides a comparison of the estimates.

title: 8  Replication: Bakker, Lelkes, & Malka (2021)
section: 8.7 The Factor Structure of the Big Five
text:
8.7 The Factor Structure of the Big Five
We fit a confirmatory five-factor model spanning the full Big Five — Extraversion, Agreeableness, Conscientiousness, Neuroticism, and Openness — each measured by its 10 IPIP-50 items. Factors are allowed to correlate freely. Each item is constrained to load only on its expected (a priori) factor; no cross-loadings are estimated.
The model fits reasonably well. The CFI and TLI are on the low end, but the RMSEA is good.

big5_items <- list(
    Extraversion = c(
        "life_party", "dont_talk", "comfortable", "stay_in_back",
        "start_convos", "say_little", "talk_at_parties",
        "avoid_attention", "like_attention", "quiet"
    ),
    Agreeableness = c(
        "no_concern", "interest_people", "insult_people",
        "sympathize", "no_care_probs", "soft_heart",
        "no_interest_probs", "time_for_others", "feel_emotions",
        "others_at_ease"
    ),
    Conscientiousness = c(
        "always_prepared", "leave_belongings", "pay_attention",
        "make_mess", "chores_done", "forget_to_put", "like_order",
        "shirk_duties", "follow_sched", "exacting"
    ),
    Neuroticism = c(
        "stress_easy", "relaxed", "worry", "easy_disturb",
        "upset_easily", "seldom_blue", "often_blue", "change_mood",
        "mood_swings", "irritate_easy"
    ),
    Openness = c(
        "rich_vocab", "dffclt_undrstnd", "vivid_imagin", "no_interest",
        "excellent_ideas", "poor_imagin", "quick_undrstnd",
        "dffclt_words", "reflecting", "full_of_ideas"
    )
)

cfa_syntax <- "
Extraversion =~ life_party + dont_talk + comfortable + stay_in_back +
    start_convos + say_little + talk_at_parties + avoid_attention +
    like_attention + quiet
Agreeableness =~ no_concern + interest_people + insult_people + sympathize +
    no_care_probs + soft_heart + no_interest_probs + time_for_others +
    feel_emotions + others_at_ease
Conscientiousness =~ always_prepared + leave_belongings + pay_attention + make_mess +
    chores_done + forget_to_put + like_order + shirk_duties + follow_sched + exacting
Neuroticism =~ stress_easy + relaxed + worry + easy_disturb + upset_easily +
    seldom_blue + often_blue + change_mood + mood_swings + irritate_easy
Openness =~ rich_vocab + dffclt_undrstnd + vivid_imagin + no_interest +
    excellent_ideas + poor_imagin + quick_undrstnd + dffclt_words +
    reflecting + full_of_ideas
Closed =~ Openness + Conscientiousness
"

big5_names <- unlist(big5_items, use.names = FALSE)

cfa_dat <- liss_long |>
    select(all_of(big5_names))

cfa_fit <- cfa(cfa_syntax,
    data = cfa_dat,
    missing = "fiml",
    std.lv = TRUE
)

cat("n_obs =", nrow(cfa_dat),
    "; n_complete =", sum(complete.cases(cfa_dat)),
    "; n_miss =", sum(is.na(cfa_dat)),
    "\n")

fitMeasures(cfa_fit, c(
    "cfi", "tli", "rmsea", "rmsea.ci.lower",
    "rmsea.ci.upper", "srmr"
)) |>
    round(3) |>
    as.list() |>
    as_tibble() |>
    knitr::kable(caption = "Hierarchical CFA (Big Five + higher-order Closed): global fit.")

standardizedSolution(cfa_fit) |>
    filter(op == "=~") |>
    select(factor = lhs, item = rhs, est.std, se, pvalue) |>
    mutate(across(c(est.std, se), \(x) round(x, 3)),
        pvalue = signif(pvalue, 2)
    ) |>
    knitr::kable(caption = "Standardized loadings (each item on its expected factor).")

standardizedSolution(cfa_fit) |>
    filter(op == "~~", lhs != rhs,
           lhs %in% c(names(big5_items), "Closed"),
           rhs %in% c(names(big5_items), "Closed")) |>
    select(factor1 = lhs, factor2 = rhs, est.std, se, pvalue) |>
    mutate(across(c(est.std, se), \(x) round(x, 3)),
        pvalue = signif(pvalue, 2)
    ) |>
    knitr::kable(caption = "Standardized factor correlations/covariances among latent factors.")


8.7.1 Estimates for Each Big Five Personality Trait
We now repeat the CLPM and RI-CLPM analyses for each Big Five trait individually (not just the closed-personality composite) against all five political items, and report ICCs for each trait.
